summary: I was born and raised in Mount Sterling, Kentucky, and my family has been here for generations. Growing up, Court Day was a big event in our town, and I have fond memories of wandering downtown with friends during those days in the early 1980s. Mount Sterling is where my family settled after moving through nearby counties, and it holds a special place in my heart as both my home and the center of our long local history.

My family has been part of Montgomery County for nearly ninety years in the public school system, starting in 1934. I taught social studies here for 28 years before retiring in 2023, continuing a tradition started by my mother and grandmother. Montgomery County is not just where I live but where my ancestors have deep roots, including connections to early frontier events like the Morgan Station attack.

Kentucky is where my family’s story unfolds, with ancestors moving through various counties like Bath, Nicholas, and Mason before settling in Montgomery County. Back in the early 1800s, my Manley ancestors came to Kentucky, and my grandmother taught in rural Kentucky schools before college degrees were mandatory. Kentucky’s history, from frontier times to modern days, is deeply intertwined with my family’s experiences and my own teaching career.

My dad’s family originally came from Bath County, near Sharpsburg, just a few miles from Montgomery County. Over time, the Manleys moved south from Mason County through Bath County before settling in Montgomery. Bath County is part of the broader region where my ancestors lived and worked, connecting our family story across these neighboring counties.

Sharpsburg is a small town near Bath County where part of my dad’s family lived before moving to Montgomery County. It’s just a few miles away, and it represents one of the early places my ancestors called home. Although I grew up in Mount Sterling, Sharpsburg is part of the regional history that shaped my family’s journey in Kentucky.

My grandmother was originally from Nicholas County, Kentucky, which is part of our family’s heritage. The Caudill side of my family moved from eastern Kentucky counties like Breathitt and Letcher into Nicholas before coming to Mount Sterling. Nicholas County is a key piece of our family’s migration story within Kentucky.

Breathitt County is one of the eastern Kentucky counties where my mother’s family, the Caudills, originally came from before moving westward. It’s part of the deeper roots of my family’s history in Kentucky, connecting us to the Appalachian region. Though we eventually settled in Montgomery County, Breathitt County remains an important ancestral place.

Letcher County is another eastern Kentucky area tied to my mother’s Caudill family heritage. Like Breathitt County, it represents the Appalachian roots of my ancestors before they moved toward central Kentucky. These counties are part of the broader story of how my family came to settle in Montgomery County.

Morgan Station is a significant place in my family history because in 1794, my sixth or seventh great-grandmother was murdered there during a Shawnee attack. My ancestor George Becraft was kidnapped at Morgan Station and taken to Detroit before being released after the Battle of Fallen Timbers. This event connects my family directly to the early frontier conflicts in Kentucky and the struggles of settlers in that era.

The Shawnee were the Native American group responsible for the attack on Morgan Station in 1794 where my ancestor’s family suffered greatly. They kidnapped my ancestor George Becraft during that raid, which was a pivotal event in our family story. The Shawnee’s role in early Kentucky history is a reminder of the conflicts settlers faced on the frontier.

George Becraft is one of my ancestors who was kidnapped by the Shawnee at Morgan Station in 1794 and taken to Detroit. He was sold to a Scottish merchant who taught him to read and write, and he was released after the Battle of Fallen Timbers. George eventually made his way back to his father, Abraham Becraft, in Kentucky, making his story a remarkable part of my family history.

Detroit is where my ancestor George Becraft was taken after being kidnapped by the Shawnee in 1794. He was sold to a Scottish merchant there who taught him literacy skills before he was released following the Battle of Fallen Timbers. Detroit represents the distant place where part of my family’s early American story unfolded far from Kentucky.

The Battle of Fallen Timbers in 1794 was a turning point that led to the release of captives like my ancestor George Becraft. After this battle, George was freed and able to return to his family in Kentucky. This event is a key historical moment tied to my family’s early frontier experiences and the broader struggle for control in the region.

Abraham Becraft was the father of George Becraft and remained in Kentucky while his son was kidnapped and taken to Detroit. George eventually returned to Abraham after being released following the Battle of Fallen Timbers. Abraham represents the family’s presence in Kentucky during those turbulent frontier years.

My Manley ancestors first settled in Mason County, Kentucky, in the early 1800s before gradually moving south into Fleming, Bath, and finally Montgomery County. Mason County is the starting point of our family’s journey through Kentucky. It’s part of the layered migration story that shaped where we live today.

Doris Nolan is my aunt and the family genealogist who sparked my interest in tracing our ancestry. She began researching our family history back in the 1950s, long before the internet made it easier. Her dedication laid the groundwork for my own genealogical work, especially during the pandemic when I dove deeper into our family’s past.

Ancestry.com was a valuable tool I used to advance our family research, especially when we hit roadblocks. It helped us confirm connections through DNA testing, like identifying the father of my great-grandmother who was born out of wedlock in Carter County. Using Ancestry.com brought new clarity and evidence to stories passed down through oral tradition.

Carter County is where my great-grandmother was born out of wedlock, and family stories always hinted at who her father was. Through genealogical research and DNA testing, we were able to connect with descendants of that family and confirm those oral traditions. Carter County is an important piece of our family puzzle and history.

Court Day in Mount Sterling has been a long-standing festival since 1794, and it was a highlight of my youth in the early 1980s. It was a time when school was out, and my friends and I could roam downtown freely, hunting for baseball cards and comic books among the vendors. The event brought the community together, with crowds sometimes over 100,000 people, creating a sense of freedom and excitement for us kids.

Main Street in Mount Sterling was a central part of Court Day celebrations, with vendors filling the city parking lot between Main Street and Locust Street. As a kid, I spent hours wandering around Main Street during Court Day, exploring stands and meeting friends. It was the heart of the festival and a place where the community gathered in large numbers.

Locust Street, alongside Main Street, was part of the area filled with vendors during Court Day in Mount Sterling. The parking lot between these streets was bustling with activity, and I remember spending a lot of time there as a child during the festival. Locust Street helped frame the central area where the event took place.

McCormick Lumber was a landmark near the area where Court Day vendors set up along Queen Street in Mount Sterling. The gravel area by the railroad tracks near McCormick Lumber was filled with booths during the festival. It was part of the extended space that made Court Day such a large and vibrant event.

Kiwanis International was mentioned as one of the organizations involved in community activities in Mount Sterling, though I didn’t discuss it much in my story. It’s part of the local civic groups that contribute to the town’s social fabric. While I focused more on family and local history, Kiwanis is one of the groups that help support community events here.

The Masonic Lodge is another community organization in Mount Sterling that has been part of the town’s social life. My family or I have some awareness of it, though it wasn’t a central part of my story. It represents one of the longstanding fraternal organizations contributing to local traditions and connections.

The Order of the Eastern Star is a fraternal organization in Mount Sterling that complements groups like the Masonic Lodge. While I didn’t delve into it much, it’s part of the community network in town. These organizations help maintain social ties and support local events in our area.

I remember during a school trip to Washington, D.C., around 1982, one of my friends brought a switchblade comb that looked like a knife to the White House tour. The security guards reacted strongly and confiscated the comb, which was quite a memorable moment for us. The White House visit was a highlight of that trip, contrasting with the small-town freedom we enjoyed back home in Mount Sterling.

I took a school trip to Washington, D.C., in the early 1980s, which was a big deal for us. During the trip, we toured the White House, and one of my friends got into trouble for bringing a switchblade comb that security mistook for a knife. It was a memorable experience that contrasted with the freedom we had growing up in Mount Sterling during events like Court Day.
